hypothesis: Bollinger Band mean reversion applied to a basket of 8 liquid ETFs exploits short-term liquidity dislocations and overreaction. When price crosses below the lower band (SMA - 2σ), it signals a statistically extreme deviation that tends to revert within days. Key learnings from prior iterations: (1) RSI reversal (iter 1) had the right frequency and OOS characteristics but failed on IS edge — Bollinger Bands use a wider signal window (20 days) that should produce stronger per-trade edge; (2) Daily/weekly momentum strategies generated too few trades or negative Sharpe from shorting — this strategy is long-only, avoiding the short-side losses from iter 4; (3) Mean reversion on ETFs has known IS Sharpe ~1.1 per knowledge base. The strategy buys when price crosses below lower Bollinger Band (2σ) and exits when price crosses back above the middle band (SMA). Applied across 8 ETFs daily, this generates 50-150 trades/year with sufficient per-trade edge. Long-only avoids borrow costs and short-side whipsaw. The ETF basket diversifies idiosyncratic risk. 20-day window balances signal quality vs. responsiveness.

evaluation.post_mortem: This backtest reveals a deeply concerning inversion of the typical overfitting pattern — OOS dramatically outperforms IS (OOS Sharpe 0.96 vs IS Sharpe 0.039, post-cost: 0.89 vs 0.003). While the usual concern is IS >> OOS, a severe IS << OOS divergence is equally suspicious and suggests the IS period may have been a structurally unfavorable regime for this strategy (e.g., trending or high-volatility environment), or the OOS period coincidentally aligned with ideal mean-reversion conditions. Either way, this large gap signals unreliable strategy behavior across regimes. Additionally, several hard acceptance criteria were missed: (1) IS Sharpe of 0.039 badly fails the ≥1.0 requirement; (2) Post-cost IS Sharpe of 0.003 barely survives but is economically meaningless; (3) Total IS trades of 24 fails the ≥50 minimum, meaning the IS results are statistically underpowered — the profit factor of 0.99 on only 24 trades confirms there is no real IS edge; (4) OOS trades of only 8.75 further underscore the low-frequency problem. Walk-forward passed 3/7 windows (only 43% pass rate), though the consistency score of 1.0 is noted. The OOS max drawdown is attractive at -7.2%, but OOS statistics built on ~9 trades are not meaningful. The strategy's hypothesis is sound in principle, but execution and regime-matching are clearly broken in the IS period.
evaluation.next_direction: Address the critical trade frequency problem first — 24 IS trades and 8.75 OOS trades are far too few for statistical validity and fail the 50-trade minimum. Consider: (1) Expanding the ETF basket from 8 to 15-20 instruments to multiply signal opportunities; (2) Loosening the entry threshold from 2.0σ to 1.5σ to capture more crossings while retaining mean-reversion logic; (3) Shortening the BB window from 20 to 10-15 days to increase signal frequency; (4) Investigate why IS Sharpe is near zero while OOS is ~1.0 — examine whether IS covered a trending bull/bear market regime where mean reversion underperforms, and consider regime-filtering (e.g., only trade when VIX > threshold or market breadth is neutral). The IS edge must be demonstrated before OOS performance can be trusted.
